objectives.text: Differentiate execution modes to optimize job queuing
Run quantum circuits with a real hardware in the IBM Runtime provider

questions.stem: A researcher needs to run 30 circuits that are fully independent of each other (no circuit depends on another's results) on a busy IBM Quantum device, and wants to minimize total time spent waiting in the queue. Which execution mode fits best?

options.text: Batch mode — submit the 30 jobs together as one batch
Session mode — open a session and submit the 30 jobs inside it
Job mode — submit 30 separate individual jobs
One session per circuit — open 30 sessions, one job each

explanation.correct: Batch mode is designed exactly for collections of independent jobs: the jobs are scheduled as a group, can run back-to-back with parallelized classical processing, and the whole collection clears the queue faster than individually queued jobs — without reserving the device between jobs the way a session does.

distractors.B: Sessions give you dedicated, iterative access — useful when later jobs depend on earlier results (e.g. variational loops). For independent circuits the dedicated window buys nothing and typically costs more device time than batching.
distractors.C: Individually queued jobs each wait in the fair-share queue separately — this maximizes, not minimizes, total queuing overhead.
distractors.D: Opening a session per circuit multiplies session overhead thirty-fold and is the most wasteful option of all.

questions.stem: You are running a variational algorithm: each iteration submits one Estimator job whose circuit parameters are chosen by a classical optimizer from the previous iteration's expectation value. You want to avoid re-queuing between iterations and keep the QPU responsive to the tight classical-quantum feedback loop. Which execution mode is the best fit?

options.text: Batch mode
Session mode
Job mode (submit each iteration as a separate individual job)
Combine all iterations into a single primitive job

explanation.correct: A session gives dedicated, exclusive access to the QPU for a window, so an iterative workload whose next input depends on the previous result runs back-to-back without each job re-entering the fair-share queue. This is the documented use case for sessions: iterative workloads requiring frequent classical-quantum communication.

distractors.A: Batch mode is for independent jobs that are all available at the outset; here each iteration's parameters depend on the previous result, so the jobs cannot be submitted together and batching provides no benefit.
distractors.C: Submitting each iteration as a separate job sends every iteration back through the queue, maximizing wall-clock time and device drift — the opposite of the goal.
distractors.D: The iterations cannot be one job: iteration n+1's parameters are computed classically from iteration n's result, so they are unknown when the job would be submitted.

questions.stem: You want to compare the effect of several different error-mitigation settings on one circuit and observable. The jobs are independent, all known at the outset, and you want them to run as closely together in time as possible (to limit device drift) while still being able to inspect each job's result separately. Which execution mode does the documentation recommend?

options.text: Job mode, submitting all jobs and hoping the scheduler groups them
Session mode
Batch mode
A separate session per mitigation setting

explanation.correct: All jobs are independent and available at the outset, so they can be scheduled collectively in a batch: you wait through the queue only once, the classical pre-processing runs in parallel, and the jobs run closely together to limit drift, while you still get each job's result individually. Because sessions are generally more expensive, batch is preferred whenever dedicated access is not required.

distractors.A: Plain job mode creates no scheduling relationship between jobs — each waits in the queue separately with no guarantee they run close together.
distractors.B: A session would work but is generally more expensive; the docs recommend batch whenever you do not need a session's dedicated exclusive access.
distractors.D: Opening a session per setting multiplies session overhead and cost while giving independent jobs no benefit.

questions.stem: The circuit `qc` is built with abstract gates and passed straight to a primitive bound to a real-device backend, without any transpilation. What happens when this code runs?
questions.code:
from qiskit import QuantumCircuit
from qiskit_ibm_runtime import SamplerV2
from qiskit_ibm_runtime.fake_provider import FakeManilaV2

backend = FakeManilaV2()
qc = QuantumCircuit(2)
qc.h(0)
qc.cx(0, 1)
qc.measure_all()

sampler = SamplerV2(mode=backend)
job = sampler.run([qc])          # qc was never transpiled
print(job.result()[0].data.meas.get_counts())

options.text: It prints counts split roughly 50/50 between `'00'` and `'11'` — the primitive transpiles the circuit automatically before running.
It raises an error: the circuit's instructions do not match the backend's ISA (the `h` gate is not in the target's instruction set), so the primitive rejects it.
It raises a `TranspilerError` because no pass manager was created.
It prints counts over only `'00'`, silently dropping the gates the backend does not support.

explanation.correct: Runtime primitives require ISA circuits: the circuit must already conform to the backend's instruction set architecture (native basis gates and qubit connectivity). Passing an un-transpiled circuit raises `IBMInputValueError` (`the instruction h on qubits (0,) is not supported by the target system`). You must build a preset pass manager and transpile first.

distractors.A: V2 primitives do NOT auto-transpile for the target; you must produce an ISA circuit yourself. This is a common V1-to-V2 migration misconception.
distractors.C: The error is not a `TranspilerError` — nothing is being transpiled. The primitive itself rejects the non-ISA circuit with `IBMInputValueError`.
distractors.D: Unsupported instructions are never silently dropped; that would change the computation. The circuit is rejected outright.

evidence.B: executed snippet -> outcome=('exception', 'IBMInputValueError', "'The instruction h on qubits (0,) is not supported by the target system. Circuits that do not match the target hardware definition are no longer supported after March 4, 2024. See the transpilation do"); option B predicts 'raises: circuit not an ISA circuit' -> match=True
evidence.A: executed snippet -> outcome=('exception', 'IBMInputValueError', "'The instruction h on qubits (0,) is not supported by the target system. Circuits that do not match the target hardware definition are no longer supported after March 4, 2024. See the transpilation do"); option A predicts 'runs, counts over 00/11' -> match=False
evidence.C: executed snippet -> outcome=('exception', 'IBMInputValueError', "'The instruction h on qubits (0,) is not supported by the target system. Circuits that do not match the target hardware definition are no longer supported after March 4, 2024. See the transpilation do"); option C predicts 'raises TranspilerError' -> match=False
evidence.D: executed snippet -> outcome=('exception', 'IBMInputValueError', "'The instruction h on qubits (0,) is not supported by the target system. Circuits that do not match the target hardware definition are no longer supported after March 4, 2024. See the transpilation do"); option D predicts 'runs, counts over only 00' -> match=False

questions.stem: You have opened a batch with `batch = Batch(backend=backend)` and want to create a SamplerV2 that submits its jobs inside that batch. Which construction is correct?
questions.code:
from qiskit_ibm_runtime import SamplerV2, Batch

batch = Batch(backend=backend)
sampler = ...  # which one?

options.text: `SamplerV2(session=batch)`
`SamplerV2(batch=batch)`
`SamplerV2(mode=batch)`
`SamplerV2(mode="batch")`

explanation.correct: In V2, the execution mode is passed through the single `mode=` parameter, which accepts a Backend, Session, or Batch object. `SamplerV2(mode=batch)` binds the primitive to the batch so its jobs are scheduled together.

distractors.A: `session=` was the pre-0.24 keyword and no longer exists on V2 primitives (raises `TypeError`); you also would not pass a Batch to a `session` argument.
distractors.B: There is no `batch=` keyword on the V2 primitives; it raises `TypeError`. The execution mode always goes through `mode=`.
distractors.D: `mode=` needs the actual Batch object, not the string `"batch"`; a string raises `ValueError: mode must be of type Backend, Session, Batch or None`.

evidence.C: constructed and ran: 64 shots over 3 bitstrings
evidence.B: raised TypeError: SamplerV2.__init__() got an unexpected keyword argument 'batch'
evidence.A: raised TypeError: SamplerV2.__init__() got an unexpected keyword argument 'session'
evidence.D: raised ValueError: mode must be of type Backend, Session, Batch or None

questions.stem: Which of the following statements about batch mode are TRUE? (Select two.)

options.text: The classical pre-processing (for example, compilation) of the jobs runs in parallel, so a batch finishes significantly faster than running the same jobs serially.
Jobs in a batch are not guaranteed to run in the order they were submitted, and they do not get exclusive access to the QPU.
Batch mode reserves the QPU exclusively, so no other users' jobs or calibration jobs can run during the batch.
Wrapping a single job in a batch reduces that job's queuing time compared with job mode.
Batch mode is only available on paid plans; Open Plan users cannot submit batch jobs.

explanation.correct: Batch parallelizes the classical processing of independent jobs and packs their quantum execution tightly, so a batch completes faster than serial submission. However, batched jobs are not guaranteed to run in submission order and do NOT get exclusive access — other users' jobs and calibration jobs may run in between if there is capacity.

distractors.C: Exclusive access (no other users' jobs, no calibration jobs) is the defining feature of SESSION mode, not batch. Batch jobs share the QPU.
distractors.D: The queuing time does not decrease for the first job submitted within a batch, so batching gives no benefit for a single job.
distractors.E: It is SESSION mode that Open Plan users cannot use. Batch mode is available on the Open Plan (with a shorter maximum TTL).

questions.stem: A developer on the free Open Plan writes code that opens `Session(backend=backend)` and submits jobs inside it. What is the documented outcome, and what should they do instead?

options.text: The session runs normally — the Open Plan supports all three execution modes.
Open Plan users cannot submit session jobs; the workload must run in job mode or batch mode instead.
Sessions work on the Open Plan but are limited to a single job each.
The Open Plan supports sessions but not batch mode.

explanation.correct: The documentation states explicitly that Open Plan users cannot submit session jobs; their workloads must run in job mode or batch mode. Sessions (dedicated, exclusive access) are reserved for paid plans.

distractors.A: The Open Plan does NOT support sessions; only job and batch mode are available to it.
distractors.C: Sessions are not merely limited on the Open Plan — they cannot be submitted at all.
distractors.D: It is the reverse: batch mode IS available on the Open Plan (with a 10-minute max TTL); sessions are not.

questions.stem: This code, adapted from a Qiskit V1 tutorial, fails on the line that constructs the sampler. What is the bug?
questions.code:
from qiskit_ibm_runtime import SamplerV2
from qiskit_ibm_runtime.fake_provider import FakeManilaV2

backend = FakeManilaV2()
# isa: a circuit already transpiled for `backend`
sampler = SamplerV2(backend=backend)
job = sampler.run([isa])

options.text: `SamplerV2` has no `backend=` parameter; the execution target is passed through `mode=` — use `SamplerV2(mode=backend)`.
`SamplerV2` must be imported from `qiskit.primitives`, not `qiskit_ibm_runtime`.
The circuit must be passed to the constructor, not to `run()`.
`run()` needs the backend as a second argument: `sampler.run([isa], backend)`.

explanation.correct: In V2 the `backend=` keyword was removed from the primitive constructors; the execution mode (a Backend, Session, or Batch) is supplied through `mode=`. `SamplerV2(backend=backend)` raises `TypeError: unexpected keyword argument 'backend'`; the fix is `SamplerV2(mode=backend)`.

distractors.B: `SamplerV2` from `qiskit_ibm_runtime` is the correct Runtime primitive and works fine once `mode=` is used — the import is not the problem.
distractors.C: The circuit is passed to `run()` as a PUB, not to the constructor; `sampler.run([isa])` is correct.
distractors.D: `run()` takes only the iterable of PUBs (plus keyword options like `shots`); adding the backend as a positional argument raises `TypeError`.

evidence.A: SamplerV2(backend=backend) -> TypeError: SamplerV2.__init__() got an unexpected keyword argument 'backend'; SamplerV2(mode=backend) -> mode=backend ran (64 shots); bug is backend= and fix is mode= -> True
evidence.B: SamplerV2 imported from qiskit_ibm_runtime constructed and ran via mode= -> that import is valid, so B is false (False)
evidence.C: passing the circuit to run([isa]) works -> the circuit belongs in run(), not the constructor, so C is false (False)
evidence.D: SamplerV2(mode=backend).run([isa], backend) -> TypeError: SamplerV2.run() takes 2 positional arguments but 3 were given -> D's proposed fix is invalid (False)

questions.stem: Your workload is a set of independent circuits with no feedback between them. A colleague suggests always using a session 'to be safe'. According to IBM's guidance, why is batch usually the better default here?

options.text: Batch mode guarantees your jobs run before any other user's jobs.
Sessions cannot run more than one job, so they cannot handle a set of circuits.
Sessions are generally more expensive than batches, so you should use batch whenever you do not need a session's dedicated exclusive access.
Sessions do not support the SamplerV2 and EstimatorV2 primitives.

explanation.correct: Sessions provide dedicated, exclusive access but at higher usage cost. The docs recommend using batch whenever you don't need that dedicated access — as with a set of independent jobs — because batch delivers the same parallel classical processing and single queue wait more cheaply.

distractors.A: Batch jobs do not get priority over other users; they are not even exclusive. It is sessions that reserve the QPU.
distractors.B: Sessions are explicitly designed for multi-job workloads; a session can run many jobs.
distractors.D: Both primitives run in sessions (and in batches and job mode); the mode is passed via `mode=`.

questions.stem: The circuit is transpiled to the backend's ISA (which pads it to the device's qubit count), but the two-qubit observable is passed to the Estimator without being mapped to the transpiled layout. What happens?
questions.code:
from qiskit import QuantumCircuit
from qiskit.quantum_info import SparsePauliOp
from qiskit.transpiler.preset_passmanagers import generate_preset_pass_manager
from qiskit_ibm_runtime import EstimatorV2
from qiskit_ibm_runtime.fake_provider import FakeManilaV2

backend = FakeManilaV2()
qc = QuantumCircuit(2); qc.h(0); qc.cx(0, 1)
obs = SparsePauliOp("ZZ")
pm = generate_preset_pass_manager(backend=backend, optimization_level=1)
isa = pm.run(qc)
estimator = EstimatorV2(mode=backend)
job = estimator.run([(isa, obs)])   # obs NOT mapped with apply_layout
print(job.result()[0].data.evs)

options.text: It prints an expectation value close to `+1`.
It raises a `ValueError`: the 2-qubit observable does not match the transpiled circuit's qubit count — the observable must be mapped with `obs.apply_layout(isa.layout)` first.
It prints an expectation value close to `0` because the observable is ignored.
It raises `IBMInputValueError` because the observable is not an ISA operator.

explanation.correct: Transpiling to the device maps the 2-qubit circuit onto the 5-qubit target, so the circuit now has 5 qubits while the observable still has 2. The Estimator raises `ValueError` (`the number of qubits of the circuit ... does not match the number of qubits of the ...-th observable`). You must align the observable with `obs.apply_layout(isa.layout)`.

distractors.A: No value is produced — the mismatch is caught before any expectation value is computed.
distractors.C: The observable is not silently ignored; a mismatched observable is a hard error.
distractors.D: The error is a plain `ValueError` about qubit counts, not `IBMInputValueError`; the failure is the observable/circuit size mismatch, not an ISA-instruction problem.

evidence.B: observed=('exception', 'ValueError', 'The number of qubits of the circuit (5) does not match the number of qubits of the ()-th observable (2).'); option B predicts 'ValueError: observable/circuit qubit-count mismatch' -> match=True
evidence.A: observed=('exception', 'ValueError', 'The number of qubits of the circuit (5) does not match the number of qubits of the ()-th observable (2).'); option A predicts 'prints evs ~ +1' -> match=False
evidence.C: observed=('exception', 'ValueError', 'The number of qubits of the circuit (5) does not match the number of qubits of the ()-th observable (2).'); option C predicts 'prints evs ~ 0' -> match=False
evidence.D: observed=('exception', 'ValueError', 'The number of qubits of the circuit (5) does not match the number of qubits of the ()-th observable (2).'); option D predicts 'IBMInputValueError' -> match=False

questions.stem: You want the Runtime service to automatically pick a real QPU that is currently operational and has the shortest queue. Which call does this?
questions.code:
from qiskit_ibm_runtime import QiskitRuntimeService
service = QiskitRuntimeService()
backend = ...  # which call?

options.text: `service.least_busy(operational=True, simulator=False)`
`service.least_busy(simulator=True)`
`service.backend()`
`service.most_available()`

explanation.correct: `least_busy` returns the backend with the fewest pending jobs. Passing `operational=True` restricts to devices that are up, and `simulator=False` restricts to real QPUs, so you get the least-busy operational hardware backend.

distractors.B: `simulator=True` would return a simulator, not a real QPU, and does not filter for operational status.
distractors.C: `service.backend()` retrieves a specific backend by name and does not consider queue length; called with no name it does not select the least busy device.
distractors.D: There is no `most_available()` method on `QiskitRuntimeService`; the selector is `least_busy`.

questions.stem: Inside a `with Batch(backend=backend):` block you construct `sampler = SamplerV2(mode=backend)` — passing the backend object rather than the batch — and submit jobs. What happens?
questions.code:
with Batch(backend=backend):
    sampler = SamplerV2(mode=backend)   # backend, not the batch
    job = sampler.run([isa])

options.text: The jobs run in batch mode because they are lexically inside the `with Batch(...)` block.
The jobs run in job mode, not batch mode — setting the mode to a backend bypasses the surrounding batch context.
It raises an error because a primitive inside a batch context must set `mode=batch`.
The batch is silently converted into a session.

explanation.correct: The execution mode is determined by the `mode` argument, not by lexical position. If you set the mode to a backend object, the program runs in job mode even inside a batch or session context. To run inside the batch, pass the batch (`mode=batch`) or construct the primitive with no mode inside the context so it inherits the batch.

distractors.A: Being inside the `with` block is not enough; passing a backend as the mode overrides the context and forces job mode.
distractors.C: It does not raise — it runs, just in job mode. A warning is logged that the job is running in job mode.
distractors.D: Nothing converts a batch to a session; the batch context is simply bypassed for that primitive.

questions.stem: A `SamplerV2` is constructed with no mode and is not inside any session or batch context, then `run` is called. What is the result?
questions.code:
from qiskit_ibm_runtime import SamplerV2
# isa: a transpiled circuit; no Session/Batch context is open
sampler = SamplerV2()          # no mode given
job = sampler.run([isa])

options.text: It raises `TypeError` because `mode` is a required positional argument.
It runs on a default local simulator.
It raises `ValueError: A backend or session must be specified.` — a primitive needs an execution target.
It automatically selects the least busy backend.

explanation.correct: `mode` defaults to `None`, so construction succeeds, but a primitive must ultimately have an execution target. With no mode and no enclosing session/batch, calling `run` raises `ValueError: A backend or session must be specified.`

distractors.A: `mode` is optional (it defaults to `None`), so construction does not raise `TypeError`; the failure happens at `run` time.
distractors.B: There is no implicit default simulator; you must supply a backend (for example, a fake backend for local testing) as the mode.
distractors.D: The primitive never contacts a service to pick a backend for you; backend selection (for example `least_busy`) is your responsibility.

evidence.C: observed=('exception', 'ValueError', 'A backend or session must be specified.'); option C predicts 'ValueError: a backend or session must be specified' -> match=True
evidence.B: observed=('exception', 'ValueError', 'A backend or session must be specified.'); option B predicts 'runs on a default local simulator' -> match=False
evidence.A: observed=('exception', 'ValueError', 'A backend or session must be specified.'); option A predicts 'TypeError: mode is a required positional argument' -> match=False
evidence.D: observed=('exception', 'ValueError', 'A backend or session must be specified.'); option D predicts 'auto-selects the least busy backend' -> match=False

questions.stem: Which TWO statements correctly describe what happens when you CLOSE a session (for example, by exiting the `with Session(...)` context manager)? (Select two.)

options.text: Closing resets the session's maximum TTL back to zero and reopens it.
Jobs already submitted (running or queued) continue to completion, and their results can still be retrieved.
All queued jobs are immediately cancelled and their results are lost.
The session stops accepting new jobs.
The backend is removed from the service and must be re-fetched.

explanation.correct: Closing a session puts it into 'In progress, not accepting new jobs': it stops accepting new submissions but finishes processing jobs that are already running or queued, and you can still retrieve their results. Exiting the context manager is equivalent to `session.close()`.

distractors.A: Closing does not reset the TTL or reopen the session; a closed session cannot accept new jobs.
distractors.C: Immediate cancellation of queued jobs is what CANCELLING a session (or hitting the maximum TTL) does — not closing it.
distractors.E: Closing a session has no effect on the backend object or the service.

questions.stem: A developer expects `counts` to hold measurement results, but the last line fails with an error and the result data is empty. What is the bug?
questions.code:
qc = QuantumCircuit(2)
qc.h(0)
qc.cx(0, 1)
# (no measurement instructions)
isa = pm.run(qc)
job = SamplerV2(mode=backend).run([isa], shots=1024)
counts = job.result()[0].data.meas.get_counts()   # fails / empty

options.text: The circuit has no measurement instructions, so the Sampler returns an empty result with no `meas` register; add `qc.measure_all()` (or explicit measurements) before transpiling.
`get_counts()` was removed in V2; you must use `job.result().counts`.
`shots=1024` is too low to populate the classical register.
The Sampler cannot run an entangling circuit; you must use the Estimator instead.

explanation.correct: The SamplerV2 returns the outcomes of classical registers written by measurements. With no measurement, the result `DataBin` has no `meas` field, so `data.meas` raises `AttributeError` and the result is empty. Adding `qc.measure_all()` (which also creates the `meas` register) before transpiling fixes it.

distractors.B: `get_counts()` is available on the `BitArray` returned for a measured register; the fixed circuit's result uses it successfully.
distractors.C: Shot count is irrelevant here; the same `shots=1024` works as soon as a measurement is present. The problem is that nothing is measured.
distractors.D: The Sampler runs entangling circuits fine; it just needs measurements. The Estimator is for expectation values, a different task.

evidence.A: no-measure result data fields=[], meas access -> AttributeError; after measure_all Sampler returns 256 shots -> bug is the missing measurement (True)
evidence.B: get_counts() works on the fixed circuit's result -> it was not removed in V2 (False)
evidence.C: the same shots=256 succeed once a measurement is added -> shots were not the problem (False)
evidence.D: SamplerV2 returns counts for this circuit once it is measured -> the Estimator is not required (False)

questions.stem: You plan an iterative workload that uses Probabilistic Error Cancellation (PEC), where a learned noise model should stay valid across many jobs. Why is a session particularly suited to this, compared with batch or job mode?

options.text: Sessions automatically re-learn the noise model before every single job, which batch cannot do.
Only sessions support error mitigation; batch and job mode disable PEC entirely.

In a dedicated session the jobs run close together, so a noise model learned once stays valid for subsequent jobs; in batch or job mode the next job may sit in the queue long enough that the noise model becomes stale.
PEC requires exclusive access because it physically recalibrates the QPU hardware between jobs.

explanation.correct: A session's dedicated window keeps jobs running tightly together, so you can learn the noise model once and reuse it while it is still accurate. In batch or job mode a later job can spend so long in the queue that the device drifts and the learned model becomes stale.

distractors.A: The advantage is learning the model ONCE and reusing it, not re-learning it before every job.
distractors.B: Error mitigation options are available in all execution modes; the mode affects scheduling and how long a learned model stays valid, not whether mitigation is allowed.
distractors.D: PEC is a software post-processing technique; it does not recalibrate hardware. The benefit of a session is timing, not physical recalibration.

questions.stem: When you construct `QiskitRuntimeService()` without specifying a `channel`, which channel is used by default?

options.text: `ibm_cloud`
`ibm_quantum_platform`
`ibm_quantum`
`local`

explanation.correct: The default channel is `ibm_quantum_platform`. Because this is almost always the appropriate channel, the docs omit it from the examples; you only pass `channel=` to override it.

distractors.A: `ibm_cloud` is a separate channel value, not the default.
distractors.C: `ibm_quantum` was the legacy channel used by the older platform; it is not the current default and this is a common out-of-date assumption.
distractors.D: `local` is a real channel for local testing (`QiskitRuntimeService(channel="local")`), but it is not the default; the default is `ibm_quantum_platform`. You get the local channel only by requesting it explicitly, so `local` is never the default.

questions.stem: FakeManilaV2 has five qubits connected in a line (0-1-2-3-4). The circuit below uses only the backend's native basis gates, but qubits 0 and 4 are not directly connected. It is submitted without routing. What happens?
questions.code:
backend = FakeManilaV2()          # line topology 0-1-2-3-4
qc = QuantumCircuit(5)
qc.sx(0)
qc.rz(0.7, 0)
qc.cx(0, 4)                       # 0 and 4 are NOT adjacent
qc.measure_all()
job = SamplerV2(mode=backend).run([qc])

options.text: It raises a `TypeError` because a 5-qubit circuit is too large for FakeManilaV2.
It runs fine; using only the backend's basis gates is sufficient, so connectivity does not matter.
It runs after the primitive automatically inserts SWAP gates to route the CX.
It raises an error: `cx` on qubits (0, 4) is not supported because those qubits are not connected — the circuit still must be routed to the coupling map, not just expressed in basis gates.

explanation.correct: An ISA circuit must satisfy BOTH the native basis gates AND the device's qubit connectivity. Using only basis gates is not enough: a two-qubit gate between non-adjacent qubits raises `IBMInputValueError` (`the instruction cx on qubits (0, 4) is not supported by the target system`). A preset pass manager would route it by inserting SWAPs.

distractors.A: FakeManilaV2 has five qubits, so a 5-qubit circuit fits; the problem is the non-adjacent CX, not the circuit width.
distractors.B: Connectivity absolutely matters; a CX between unconnected qubits is not executable and is rejected.
distractors.C: The primitive does NOT auto-route; routing (SWAP insertion) is the transpiler's job, which you must run before submitting.

evidence.D: observed=('exception', 'IBMInputValueError', "'The instruction cx on qubits (0, 4) is not supported by the target system. Circuits that do not match the target hardware definition are no longer supported after March 4, 2024. See the transpilation"); option D predicts 'raises: cx (0,4) not supported (connectivity)' -> match=True
evidence.B: observed=('exception', 'IBMInputValueError', "'The instruction cx on qubits (0, 4) is not supported by the target system. Circuits that do not match the target hardware definition are no longer supported after March 4, 2024. See the transpilation"); option B predicts 'runs fine, basis gates suffice' -> match=False
evidence.C: observed=('exception', 'IBMInputValueError', "'The instruction cx on qubits (0, 4) is not supported by the target system. Circuits that do not match the target hardware definition are no longer supported after March 4, 2024. See the transpilation"); option C predicts 'runs after auto-inserting SWAPs' -> match=False
evidence.A: observed=('exception', 'IBMInputValueError', "'The instruction cx on qubits (0, 4) is not supported by the target system. Circuits that do not match the target hardware definition are no longer supported after March 4, 2024. See the transpilation"); option A predicts 'raises TypeError, circuit too large' -> match=False

questions.stem: A session (or batch) reaches its maximum time to live (max TTL) while some of its jobs are still running and others are still waiting in the queue. What happens?

options.text: The session is automatically extended until the queue empties.
All jobs, running and queued, are immediately cancelled.
The max TTL timer pauses until all queued jobs complete.
Jobs that are already running finish, but jobs still queued are failed/cancelled.

explanation.correct: When the maximum TTL is reached the session/batch is closed. Jobs that are already running are allowed to finish, but any jobs still queued are failed. The max TTL timer starts when the first job starts and does not pause.

distractors.A: There is no automatic extension; you set the limit with `max_time`, and reaching it ends the workload.
distractors.B: Running jobs are not killed mid-execution; only the still-queued jobs are failed.
distractors.C: The max TTL timer does not pause or stop once started; it runs to the configured limit.

questions.stem: Which snippet correctly opens a session on `backend`, runs a transpiled circuit `isa` with a Sampler inside it, and retrieves the counts?
questions.code: # backend is a real-device (fake) backend; isa is transpiled for it

options.text:
with Session(backend=backend) as session:
    job = SamplerV2(mode=session).run(isa)        # unwrapped
counts = job.result()[0].data.meas.get_counts()

with Session(backend=backend) as session:
    job = SamplerV2(mode=session).run([isa])
counts = job.result()[0].data.meas.get_counts()

session = Session(mode=backend)                    # mode=, not backend=
job = SamplerV2(mode=session).run([isa])
counts = job.result()[0].data.meas.get_counts()

with Session(backend) as session:
    job = SamplerV2(session).run(isa)             # unwrapped
counts = job.result()[0].data.meas.get_counts()

explanation.correct: `Session` takes the backend (`Session(backend=backend)`), the primitive binds to the session with `mode=session`, and `run` takes an iterable of PUBs, so the circuit is wrapped in a list: `run([isa])`. Exiting the context manager closes the session while the submitted job runs to completion.

distractors.A: `run(isa)` passes an unwrapped circuit; `run` needs an iterable of PUBs, so this raises `ValueError` about an invalid Sampler pub-like.
distractors.C: `Session` has no `mode=` parameter — the QPU is passed as `backend=`. `Session(mode=backend)` raises `TypeError`.
distractors.D: `Session(backend)` and `SamplerV2(session)` are fine positionally, but `run(isa)` is again unwrapped and raises `ValueError`.

evidence.A: raised ValueError: An invalid Sampler pub-like was given (<class 'qiskit._accelerate.circuit.CircuitInstruction'>). If you want to run a si
evidence.C: raised TypeError: Session.__init__() got an unexpected keyword argument 'mode'
evidence.D: raised ValueError: An invalid Sampler pub-like was given (<class 'qiskit._accelerate.circuit.CircuitInstruction'>). If you want to run a si

questions.stem: You partition a long list of independent circuits into several jobs and run them in batch mode. Besides shorter queuing, what practical advantage does this give over submitting everything as one giant job?

options.text: You get each job's results individually, so you can cancel the remaining jobs if early results are unsatisfactory, or re-submit only the one job that failed.
Batch mode guarantees exclusive access to the QPU for the whole run.
Batch mode applies stronger error mitigation than a single job.
A batch never counts against your usage or cost.

explanation.correct: Partitioning into multiple batched jobs makes results more flexible to work with: you receive each job's result separately, so you can stop early by cancelling remaining jobs, or re-run just a failed job instead of the entire workload.

distractors.B: Batch mode does not give exclusive access — that is a session feature. Batched jobs share the QPU.
distractors.C: Error mitigation is controlled by primitive options, not by the execution mode; batching does not change it.
distractors.D: Batched jobs still consume QPU usage and count against cost; batching does not make execution free.

questions.stem: On a trusted laptop, you want to authenticate once and then reuse your credentials in later Python sessions without pasting your API key each time. Which approach matches the documented workflow?

options.text: Store the backend name in `save_account` so that `least_busy` is no longer needed.
Pass `token=` and `instance=` to `QiskitRuntimeService()` every time; there is no way to persist them.
Call `QiskitRuntimeService.save_account(token="<api-key>", instance="<CRN>")` once; afterwards `service = QiskitRuntimeService()` loads the saved credentials.
Call `QiskitRuntimeService.login()` with your IBM id and password.

explanation.correct: `QiskitRuntimeService.save_account(token=..., instance=...)` saves credentials to disk once (recommended only in a trusted environment). Later, `QiskitRuntimeService()` with no arguments loads the saved default account, so you never paste the key again.

distractors.A: `save_account` stores authentication credentials, not a backend choice; you still select a backend (for example with `least_busy`) at run time.
distractors.B: Explicit per-session credentials are one option, but credentials CAN be saved with `save_account`; claiming there is no way to persist them is wrong.
distractors.D: There is no `login()` method that takes an id and password; authentication uses an API key/token, optionally saved with `save_account`.

questions.stem: For which TWO of the following workloads is SESSION mode the most appropriate execution mode? (Select two.)

options.text: Fifty independent circuits, all known upfront, that you want to clear the queue as cheaply as possible.
An experiment that needs the QPU reserved exclusively so that no other users' jobs or calibrations interleave with your circuits.
A variational eigensolver whose next circuit depends on the classical optimizer's use of the previous result.
A single circuit you want to run once as quickly as possible.
A large collection of unrelated benchmarking circuits where minimizing cost is the priority.

explanation.correct: Sessions shine for iterative workloads whose inputs are not all ready at the outset (A) and for experiments that need dedicated, exclusive access to the QPU (B). Both justify a session's higher cost.

distractors.A: Independent circuits known upfront that you want to run cheaply are the classic case for batch mode, which is generally less expensive than a session.
distractors.D: A single circuit run should use job mode; a session gives no queuing benefit for the first (only) job and costs more.
distractors.E: Cost-sensitive, independent benchmarking circuits belong in batch mode, not a more expensive session.

questions.stem: A developer has a backend name as a string and writes `SamplerV2(mode="ibm_manila")`, expecting the primitive to resolve it. What is the result?
questions.code:
from qiskit_ibm_runtime import SamplerV2
sampler = SamplerV2(mode="ibm_manila")

options.text: It works only if a `QiskitRuntimeService` has already been initialized in the session.
It works: the primitive looks up the backend by that name.
It raises `ValueError` — `mode` must be a `Backend`, `Session`, or `Batch` object (or `None`), not a string.
It raises `KeyError` because no backend named `'ibm_manila'` exists.

explanation.correct: SamplerV2's `mode` accepts a Backend, Session, or Batch object (or None). A string is rejected at construction with `ValueError: mode must be of type Backend, Session, Batch or None`. You must pass the resolved backend object (for example from `service.backend("ibm_manila")` or `service.least_busy(...)`).

distractors.A: The type check on `mode` runs at construction regardless of whether a service exists, so a string always raises — a service does not make it valid.
distractors.B: The primitive does not resolve backend names; that is the service's job. A string is never a valid `mode`.
distractors.D: No name lookup is attempted, so there is no `KeyError`; the type check rejects the string first with `ValueError`.

evidence.C: observed=('exception', 'ValueError', 'mode must be of type Backend, Session, Batch or None'); option C predicts 'ValueError: mode must be Backend/Session/Batch or None' -> match=True
evidence.B: observed=('exception', 'ValueError', 'mode must be of type Backend, Session, Batch or None'); option B predicts 'works, looks up by name' -> match=False
evidence.A: observed=('exception', 'ValueError', 'mode must be of type Backend, Session, Batch or None'); option A predicts 'works only if a service exists' -> match=False
evidence.D: observed=('exception', 'ValueError', 'mode must be of type Backend, Session, Batch or None'); option D predicts 'KeyError: no such backend' -> match=False

questions.stem: In a session, what is the difference between the interactive TTL and the maximum TTL?

options.text: The interactive TTL is what you configure with `max_time`; the maximum TTL cannot be set.
The interactive TTL caps the session's total lifetime; the maximum TTL is the time allowed for any single job to run.
They measure the same thing; 'interactive' is simply the Open Plan name for the maximum TTL.
The maximum TTL caps the session's total lifetime from when it starts; the interactive TTL is the idle gap allowed between consecutive jobs before the session is temporarily deactivated.

explanation.correct: The maximum TTL is the total time the session may run, measured from when the first job starts; you set it with `max_time`. The interactive TTL is the allowed idle time between jobs — if no job is queued within that window, the session is temporarily deactivated (and must be reactivated through the normal queue). The interactive TTL cannot be configured.

distractors.A: It is the MAXIMUM TTL that you set with `max_time`; the interactive TTL is fixed and not configurable — the opposite of this statement.
distractors.B: This reverses the two: the maximum TTL is the total lifetime, not a per-job limit, and the interactive TTL is the inter-job idle gap.
distractors.C: They are distinct timers with different roles; 'interactive' is not an alias for the maximum TTL.

questions.stem: You want to run a transpiled circuit as a single standalone job (job mode) on `backend`. Which construction is correct?
questions.code:
from qiskit_ibm_runtime import SamplerV2
# backend is a real-device (fake) backend
sampler = ...  # which one?

options.text: `SamplerV2(session=backend)`
`SamplerV2(backend=backend)`
`SamplerV2(mode="ibm_manila")`
`SamplerV2(mode=backend)`

explanation.correct: Job mode is achieved by giving the primitive a backend as its mode: `SamplerV2(mode=backend)`. A primitive whose mode is a backend (rather than a Session or Batch) submits a single standalone job.

distractors.A: `session=` is a removed pre-0.24 keyword and raises `TypeError`; job mode uses `mode=backend`, not a session anyway.
distractors.B: `backend=` was removed from the V2 constructors; it raises `TypeError`. Use `mode=`.
distractors.C: `mode` needs the backend object, not its name as a string; a string raises `ValueError`.

evidence.D: constructed and ran in job mode: 64 shots
evidence.B: raised TypeError: SamplerV2.__init__() got an unexpected keyword argument 'backend'
evidence.C: raised ValueError: mode must be of type Backend, Session, Batch or None
evidence.A: raised TypeError: SamplerV2.__init__() got an unexpected keyword argument 'session'

questions.stem: According to the documented best practices, which execution mode should you use to submit a single primitive request (one small experiment)?

options.text: Job mode (`mode=backend`, with no session or batch context).
Session mode, to guarantee it runs before other users' jobs.
Batch mode, to reduce the single job's queuing time.
It makes no difference; all three modes add exactly the same overhead for one job.

explanation.correct: The guidance is explicit: always use job mode to submit a single primitive request. Job mode is easiest for a small experiment and may even run sooner than batch.

distractors.B: A session does not give a single job priority over other users, and it costs more; its benefits appear only across multiple jobs.
distractors.C: The queuing time does not decrease for the first job in a batch, so batching one job gives no benefit.
distractors.D: It does make a difference: sessions and batches add overhead/cost without helping a single job, whereas job mode is the recommended, lightest choice.

questions.stem: This 'map the problem -> optimize -> execute' workflow skips a step and fails at `run`. Which change makes it run correctly?
questions.code:
backend = FakeManilaV2()
qc = QuantumCircuit(2); qc.h(0); qc.cx(0, 1)
obs = SparsePauliOp("ZZ")

estimator = EstimatorV2(mode=backend)
job = estimator.run([(qc, obs)])     # runs the abstract circuit + raw observable
print(job.result()[0].data.evs)

options.text: Transpile to an ISA circuit and map the observable to its layout before running: `isa = pm.run(qc); mapped = obs.apply_layout(isa.layout); estimator.run([(isa, mapped)])`.
Increase the shots via `estimator.options.default_shots` before running.
Wrap the circuit and observable in an extra list: `estimator.run([[qc, obs]])`.
Pass the backend as a second argument to run: `estimator.run([(qc, obs)], backend)`.

explanation.correct: The 'optimize' step is missing: the circuit was never transpiled to the backend's ISA (its `h`/`cx` are not native and unrouted), so `run` raises `IBMInputValueError`. Building an ISA circuit with the preset pass manager and mapping the observable with `apply_layout` fixes it and yields an expectation value.

distractors.B: Shot count has nothing to do with the failure; the abstract circuit is still not an ISA circuit, so raising `default_shots` still raises the same error.
distractors.C: Nesting does not transpile anything; the circuit is still non-ISA, so `run([[qc, obs]])` still raises `IBMInputValueError`.
distractors.D: `run` does not take a positional backend; adding one raises `TypeError`, and it still would not solve the missing transpilation.

evidence.A: buggy run([(qc, obs)]) -> IBMInputValueError: 'The instruction h on qubits (0,) is not supported by the target system. Circuits that do not match the target hardware ; fix transpiled + apply_layout -> evs=0.875 -> True
evidence.B: raising default_shots then run([(qc, obs)]) -> IBMInputValueError: 'The instruction h on qubits (0,) is not supported by the target system. Circuits that do  (shots do not fix a non-ISA circuit) -> False
evidence.C: run([[qc, obs]]) with the un-transpiled circuit -> IBMInputValueError: 'The instruction h on qubits (0,) is not supported by the target system. Circuits that do  -> False
evidence.D: run([(isa, mapped)], backend) extra positional -> TypeError: EstimatorV2.run() takes 2 positional arguments but 3 were given -> False

questions.stem: Your workload is a single circuit. A colleague wraps it in a batch, expecting the batch to reduce its queuing time. Why does this not help?

options.text: Single jobs submitted in a batch are always redirected to a simulator.
Batches only speed up circuits with more than 100 gates.
The batch cancels the single job because a batch requires at least two jobs.
The queuing time does not decrease for the first job submitted within a batch (or session), so wrapping a single job in a batch provides no queuing benefit.

explanation.correct: Batching helps by scheduling multiple jobs together and parallelizing their classical processing. But the first job in a batch still waits in the normal queue, so a batch (or session) provides no benefit when there is only one job.

distractors.A: Execution target is set by the mode/backend, not by whether a job is in a batch; nothing redirects it to a simulator.
distractors.B: Batch benefits come from parallel scheduling across jobs, not from circuit size; there is no gate-count threshold.
distractors.C: A batch does not require or enforce a minimum of two jobs, and it does not cancel a lone job.

questions.stem: Inside `with Session(backend=backend) as session:` you want an Estimator that submits its jobs inside the session. Which construction is correct?
questions.code:
with Session(backend=backend) as session:
    estimator = ...  # which one?

options.text: `EstimatorV2(session=session)`
`EstimatorV2(mode=session)`
`EstimatorV2(mode="session")`
`EstimatorV2(backend=session)`

explanation.correct: As with the Sampler, the execution mode is passed through `mode=`, which accepts a Session object. `EstimatorV2(mode=session)` binds the Estimator to the open session.

distractors.A: `session=` is the removed pre-0.24 keyword; on V2 primitives it raises `TypeError`. Use `mode=session`.
distractors.C: `mode` needs the Session object, not the string `"session"`; a string raises `ValueError`.
distractors.D: `backend=` was removed from the V2 constructors and raises `TypeError`; you also would not pass a Session to a `backend` argument.

evidence.B: constructed and ran in the session: evs=0.875
evidence.A: raised TypeError: EstimatorV2.__init__() got an unexpected keyword argument 'session'
evidence.C: raised ValueError: mode must be of type Backend, Session, Batch or None
evidence.D: raised TypeError: EstimatorV2.__init__() got an unexpected keyword argument 'backend'

questions.stem: Which THREE of the following statements about Qiskit Runtime execution modes are correct? (Select three.)

options.text: Job mode submits a single primitive request made without a context manager.
In session mode the user has exclusive access to the QPU during the active window; no other jobs, including calibration jobs, run.
In batch mode the classical pre-processing of the jobs is parallelized so the quantum execution can be packed tightly together.
Batch mode gives each job dedicated, exclusive QPU access, just like a session.
Session mode is available to Open Plan users, just like batch mode.
Sessions are generally cheaper than batches for the same set of independent jobs.

explanation.correct: Job mode is a single request without a context manager (A). A session grants exclusive access with no other users' or calibration jobs during the active window (B). Batch parallelizes the classical processing to pack quantum execution tightly (C).

distractors.D: Batch mode does NOT give exclusive access; batched jobs share the QPU with others. Exclusivity is a session feature.
distractors.E: Open Plan users cannot use sessions; only job and batch mode are available to them.
distractors.F: Sessions are generally MORE expensive than batches, not cheaper; batch is preferred when dedicated access is not needed.

questions.stem: You saved two accounts named `open_acct` and `premium_acct` without marking a default, then run `service = QiskitRuntimeService()` with no arguments. According to the documented behavior, which account's credentials are used?

options.text: It raises an error because no default account is set.
The account that was saved most recently.
`open_acct`, because free (Open) plans are always preferred.
The account whose name comes last alphabetically — here `premium_acct`.

explanation.correct: When multiple credential sets are saved, none is marked default, and none is specified at initialization, the service uses the one whose name comes last alphabetically. `premium_acct` sorts after `open_acct`, so `premium_acct` is used.

distractors.A: It does not raise — the alphabetical-last account is selected as the fallback.
distractors.B: Save order is not the tiebreaker; alphabetical name order is.
distractors.C: There is no automatic preference for Open/free plans when choosing among SAVED named accounts; the alphabetical rule applies.

questions.stem: When does a session (or batch) actually start — that is, when does its maximum TTL timer begin counting?

options.text: When its first job begins execution.
The moment you construct the `Session`/`Batch` object.
When you exit the context manager.
When you call `service = QiskitRuntimeService()`.

explanation.correct: A session or batch starts when its first job begins execution; that is when the maximum TTL timer starts. (Note: if you open a session but submit no jobs for 30 minutes, it closes automatically.)

distractors.B: Merely constructing the object does not start the clock — the first job entering execution does.
distractors.C: Exiting the context manager closes the session/batch; it does not start the TTL timer.
distractors.D: Creating the service only authenticates you; it is unrelated to when a session's timer begins.

questions.stem: Using local testing mode, a session is opened on FakeManilaV2 and its `backend()` accessor is called. What is printed?
questions.code:
from qiskit_ibm_runtime import Session
from qiskit_ibm_runtime.fake_provider import FakeManilaV2

backend = FakeManilaV2()
with Session(backend=backend) as session:
    print(session.backend())

options.text: `None` — a local session has no associated backend.
`<FakeManilaV2 object ...>` — it returns the backend object itself.
`fake_manila` — `session.backend()` returns the backend's name (a string).
It raises an error because no `QiskitRuntimeService` was provided.

explanation.correct: `Session.backend()` returns the name of the QPU bound to the session as a string. For a session opened on FakeManilaV2 it returns `'fake_manila'`. Local testing mode lets you open a `Session(backend=fake_backend)` and run primitives without any credentials.

distractors.A: The session does have a backend (the one you passed), so it is not `None`.
distractors.B: It returns the backend NAME (a string), not the backend object; this is a common assumption to check.
distractors.D: In local testing mode a fake backend is passed directly, so no service is needed and no error is raised.

evidence.C: session.backend() -> type=str, value='fake_manila'; option C predicts 'returns the backend name string 'fake_manila'' -> match=True
evidence.B: session.backend() -> type=str, value='fake_manila'; option B predicts 'returns the backend object' -> match=False
evidence.A: session.backend() -> type=str, value='fake_manila'; option A predicts 'returns None' -> match=False
evidence.D: session.backend() -> type=str, value='fake_manila'; option D predicts 'raises an error (no service)' -> match=False

questions.stem: You have two transpiled ISA circuits `isa1` and `isa2` and want to run both in a single Sampler job on `backend`. Which call is correct?
questions.code:
sampler = SamplerV2(mode=backend)
job = ...  # run both isa1 and isa2 in one job

options.text: `sampler.run([[isa1, isa2]])`
`sampler.run(isa1, isa2)`
`sampler.run([isa1], [isa2])`
`sampler.run([isa1, isa2])`

explanation.correct: `run` takes one iterable of PUBs. Two circuits are two PUBs, so they go in one list: `run([isa1, isa2])`. The result then has one pub-result per circuit.

distractors.A: `[[isa1, isa2]]` is a single PUB whose first element is a list of circuits — an invalid pub-like — so it raises rather than running the two circuits.
distractors.B: `run(isa1, isa2)` passes two positional arguments; `run` accepts a single `pubs` iterable, so this raises `TypeError`.
distractors.C: `run([isa1], [isa2])` again supplies two positionals (the second is interpreted as `shots`), raising an error rather than running two circuits.

evidence.D: ran, returned 2 pub result(s) in one job
evidence.B: raised TypeError: SamplerV2.run() takes 2 positional arguments but 3 were given
evidence.C: raised TypeError: SamplerV2.run() takes 2 positional arguments but 3 were given
evidence.A: raised ValueError: setting an array element with a sequence. The requested array has an inhomogeneous shape after 2 dimensions. The detecte

questions.stem: A Bell circuit is transpiled and run with the Estimator on a noisy fake backend to measure `<ZZ>`. Ideally the Bell state gives `<ZZ> = +1`. Which best describes the value actually obtained?
questions.code:
backend = FakeManilaV2()
qc = QuantumCircuit(2); qc.h(0); qc.cx(0, 1)     # Bell state
obs = SparsePauliOp("ZZ")
pm = generate_preset_pass_manager(backend=backend, optimization_level=1)
isa = pm.run(qc)
mapped = obs.apply_layout(isa.layout)
evs = EstimatorV2(mode=backend).run([(isa, mapped)]).result()[0].data.evs
print(evs)

options.text: Exactly `+1.0`, with no effect from device noise.
A value close to `0`.
A value close to `-1`.
A value close to `+1` but slightly below it (roughly `+0.9`), because the two qubits are strongly Z-correlated and noise only slightly reduces the correlation.

explanation.correct: In the Bell state the two qubits' Z outcomes are perfectly correlated, so `<ZZ>` is `+1` ideally. On a noisy device the measured value is close to but a bit below `+1` (about `+0.9` here), reflecting the small reduction from noise.

distractors.A: Real device noise reduces the magnitude below 1; you should not expect exactly +1.0 from hardware.
distractors.B: `<ZZ>` near 0 would mean the qubits are uncorrelated in Z, which is not true for a Bell state.
distractors.C: `<ZZ>` near -1 would mean anti-correlation (outcomes like 01/10 dominating); the Bell state gives 00/11, i.e. positive correlation.

evidence.D: measured <ZZ>=0.8882 on the noisy backend; option D predicts 'close to +1 but below 1 (noisy)' -> match=True
evidence.B: measured <ZZ>=0.8882 on the noisy backend; option B predicts 'close to 0' -> match=False
evidence.C: measured <ZZ>=0.8882 on the noisy backend; option C predicts 'close to -1' -> match=False
evidence.A: measured <ZZ>=0.8882 on the noisy backend; option A predicts 'exactly +1.0' -> match=False
